scenario: Quantum Bank rounds an interim balance to 4 decimal places per ledger row across 17000 transactions per day in Camunda 8.8. A FEEL expression on a business rule task multiplies the principal by a fractional rate and stores the result in a Number variable that downstream Java workers later deserialize from the engine state.
question: Which Java type does the FEEL data types reference name as the runtime type that backs a Number value at evaluation time?

options.text: The runtime type is the class java.math.BigDecimal, as recorded on the Number entry of the data types reference, so the engine uses arbitrary precision decimals for FEEL numbers
The runtime type is the primitive double, so any FEEL number is bounded by the IEEE 754 double precision floating-point range and the corresponding rounding rules
The runtime type is the class java.lang.Long, so a FEEL number that has a fractional part is truncated to an integer before being stored as a Java numeric value
The runtime type is the class java.lang.String, so the engine carries the decimal representation as text and converts it to a numeric form only when an arithmetic operator is applied

a.text: Correct. The Number entry of the data types reference records the Java type as java.math.BigDecimal, which means FEEL number values use arbitrary precision decimal arithmetic on the engine side.

b.text: Incorrect. The documented Java type for FEEL Number is java.math.BigDecimal rather than the primitive double, so IEEE 754 limits do not bound a FEEL number.

c.text: Incorrect. FEEL numbers can carry a fractional part without truncation; the documented backing type java.math.BigDecimal preserves the fractional component.

d.text: Incorrect. The data types reference records a numeric Java type for Number values rather than a string carrier, so arithmetic is applied to decimal values directly.

explanation: The FEEL data types reference records that the Number type is backed by java.math.BigDecimal at runtime, giving FEEL arithmetic arbitrary precision decimals without the IEEE 754 limits of a double.
